heroHeadline: Sheinbaum at 564 Days: Teotihuacán Mass Shooting Kills Canadian Tourist; USMCA Round 2 Advances; Chihuahua Crash Triggers Sovereignty Demand
heroSubtitle: A gunman opened fire from atop the Pyramid of the Moon on April 20, killing a 32-year-old Canadian tourist and injuring 13 others including 6 Americans — sparking a World Cup security alert as Mexico hosts three FIFA venues in June. The same day, USTR Greer met President Sheinbaum and Economy Secretary Ebrard for the second USMCA preparatory round; a joint statement set the first official bilateral negotiating session for the week of May 25. Sheinbaum also demanded an explanation after 2 U.S. Embassy officials and 2 Mexican agents died in a crash while returning from an unauthorized joint drug-lab demolition in Chihuahua, warning 'there is no permission for foreign agents to participate in operations in our country.' On April 21, the shooter was identified as having a Columbine-inspired profile. Japan PM Takaichi proposed an economic security framework in a telephone summit. Sheinbaum also announced a Gulf of Mexico permanent observatory decree to monitor Pemex-linked hydrocarbon spills.
footerNote: Data sourced from Diario Oficial, INEGI, Banco de México, SNSP, Secretaría de Economía, Reuters, AP, El Universal, Reforma, Animal Político, Mexico News Daily, Infobae, Milenio, La Jornada, Al Jazeera, Bloomberg, AtlasIntel, USTR, Chatham House, Mexico Business News, El Financiero, La Silla Rota, Proceso, CNN Español, Lopez Doriga, The Yucatan Times, Excelsior, La Razón de México, Quinto Poder, PBS NewsHour, NBC News, Time, NPR, CBC, Japan Kantei, Japan MOFA
